events.text: The patient is a 47-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African (Ghanaian) descent, born in Accra and emigrated to the United States at age 22. She is a nonsmoker with no significant alcohol use. She works as a secondary school mathematics teacher. She has two children. She has a family history notable for her maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 38 and her maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55, prompting prior genetic counseling referral.

At age 47 years, the patient received a diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of the bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (AJCC 8th edition pT3c pN1 M0). The primary site is coded as ovary (ICD-O-3 C56.9), with synchronous fallopian tube involvement (C57.0). Histology code: 8461/3 (serous cystadenocarcinoma, NOS). The tumor is high-grade (FIGO grade 3). Summary stage: regional/distant (peritoneal). Relevant staging data: ascites cytology positive for malignant cells; peritoneal carcinomatosis involving the omentum, right and left pelvic peritoneum, and multiple small bowel mesenteric implants measuring up to 2.3 cm; retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (bilateral pelvic and para-aortic nodes, largest 1.8 cm). No parenchymal liver or lung metastases. CA-125 at diagnosis: 3,847 U/mL. HE4: 412 pmol/L. Confirmation: positive histology via omentectomy specimen. Tumor size: bilateral ovarian masses, right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm.

At age 47 years, the patient had an anatomic pathology report from diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy. Gross: omental cake, 14 x 9 x 3 cm, gray-white firm tissue. Microscopic: sheets and papillary clusters of highly atypical epithelial cells with prominent nucleoli, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and abundant mitotic figures including atypical forms. Psammoma bodies present. Stromal desmoplasia noted. Immunohistochemistry: WT-1 positive (diffuse), PAX8 positive (diffuse), p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression pattern, consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation), ER weakly positive (30%), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CA-125 positive. Ki-67 proliferation index: 78%. Morphologic and IHC profile consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin. No mucinous, endometrioid, or clear cell features identified. Peritoneal washing cytology: positive for malignant epithelial cells with serous features.

At age 47 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV and oral contrast (pre-operative staging). Chest: no pleural effusion, no pulmonary nodules, no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Abdomen and pelvis: large complex right adnexal mass measuring 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm with solid and cystic components and internal septations; left adnexal mass 5.8 x 4.3 cm with similar features; moderate volume ascites; omental caking with confluent soft tissue density replacing the omentum measuring up to 11 cm in maximum dimension; multiple peritoneal implants along the right paracolic gutter, left pelvic peritoneum, and small bowel mesentery (largest 2.3 cm); right external iliac lymph node 1.6 cm, left para-aortic lymph node 1.8 cm. Liver: no parenchymal metastases, mild perihepatic ascites. Spleen: normal. No hydronephrosis. Impression: extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with bilateral adnexal masses, omental caking, and lymphadenopathy consistent with advanced ovarian primary. Disease appears surgically resectable; no suprarenal or supradiaphragmatic disease. Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) estimated at 18.

At age 47 years, the patient had germline genetic testing and tumor next-generation sequencing performed. Germline testing (blood): BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), also known as 5382insC, classified as pathogenic (ClinVar ID 37523). This is a founder mutation prevalent in populations of Eastern European Ashkenazi Jewish and also independently reported in West African populations. Secondary findings: no BRCA2 pathogenic variant identified; no Lynch syndrome gene mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) detected. Somatic tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx on omental biopsy specimen): TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%, pathogenic, gain-of-function), BRCA1 p.Gln1756ProfsTer74 (VAF 82%, consistent with loss of heterozygosity at BRCA1 locus), RB1 deep deletion (homozygous loss), CCNE1 amplification (copy number 6.2), NF1 p.R1362* (VAF 18%, subclonal, likely pathogenic), PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 12%, subclonal), NOTCH3 p.R1626C (VAF 9%, variant of uncertain significance). Tumor mutational burden: 4.2 mut/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 31% (high). HRD score: 68 (positive). BRCA1 promoter methylation: absent. No NTRK fusions detected. No AR amplification. No ERBB2 amplification. No CDK12 mutation. Actionable alterations: germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH (highly predictive of PARP inhibitor sensitivity), high HRD score.

At age 47 years, the patient had a gynecologic oncology pre-operative consultation note. The patient presented with a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. She reported bilateral lower pelvic pressure and urinary frequency. On review of systems, she noted fatigue but denied nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. Performance status: ECOG 1. Physical examination: abdomen distended with a fluid wave consistent with moderate ascites; a firm, fixed pelvic mass palpable extending to the umbilicus; no supraclavicular or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Assessment: advanced high-grade serous ovarian cancer, FIGO IIIC, with peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites. Surgical complexity score estimated as moderate-high. Based on imaging and diagnostic laparoscopy findings, patient is deemed a candidate for primary debulking surgery (PDS) with the goal of achieving optimal cytoreduction (R0). Patient counseled extensively on surgery, risks, chemotherapy, and genetic implications for family members. Patient is emotionally distressed and requires additional psychosocial support; referral placed to oncology social work and gynecologic oncology support group.

At age 47 years, the patient underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS). Procedure: exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants from the right paracolic gutter, sigmoid mesentery, and left pelvic sidewall, and diaphragmatic stripping of a 1.5 cm right hemidiaphragm implant. No bowel resection required. Estimated blood loss: 950 mL; 2 units packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 5 hours 40 minutes. Surgical findings: bilateral ovarian masses as described on imaging, moderate volume ascites (2.8 L drained and sent for cytology), omental cake, and peritoneal implants as described. At conclusion of surgery: no gross residual disease (R0 resection achieved). Surgeon's assessment: complete gross resection. Postoperative course: patient recovered in the surgical ICU overnight for monitoring, transferred to floor on postoperative day 1. No major postoperative complications. Discharged on postoperative day 5 with VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 28 days).

At age 47 years, the patient had a definitive surgical pathology report from primary debulking surgery. Right ovary (7.2 cm): high-grade serous carcinoma extensively involving the ovarian parenchyma and surface, with vascular invasion identified. Left ovary (5.8 cm): high-grade serous carcinoma with surface involvement. Right fallopian tube: serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) of the fimbriated end with invasive carcinoma involving the tubal wall, supporting fallopian tube origin of primary tumor. Left fallopian tube: STIC without invasive carcinoma. Uterus: no myometrial invasion; endometrium atrophic. Omentum: extensive replacement by metastatic high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake). Appendix: one peritoneal implant on appendiceal serosa. Right paracolic gutter peritoneum: 3 implants, largest 2.1 cm. Left pelvic sidewall peritoneum: 2 implants, largest 1.4 cm. Right hemidiaphragm peritoneum: 1 implant, 1.5 cm. Pelvic lymph nodes: 4 of 18 positive for metastatic carcinoma (right external iliac x2, left obturator x1, right obturator x1). Para-aortic lymph nodes: 1 of 9 positive (right para-aortic). Ascites cytology: positive for malignant cells. Pathologic stage: pT3c pN1 (AJCC 8th). Margins: all surgical margins negative. No residual macroscopic disease.

At age 47 years, approximately 6 weeks post-surgery, the patient had a post-operative CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (pre-chemotherapy baseline). Post-surgical changes in the pelvis including expected fluid and soft tissue stranding at the operative bed. No evidence of residual pelvic or abdominal masses. No ascites. Post-surgical lymphocele measuring 2.1 cm in the right obturator fossa, no intervention needed. No new peritoneal implants identified. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules. Impression: expected post-operative appearance following debulking surgery with no evidence of residual macroscopic disease. Appropriate baseline for monitoring response to chemotherapy.

At age 47 years, the patient had a medical oncology new patient consultation prior to initiating adjuvant chemotherapy. CA-125 at 6 weeks post-surgery: 187 U/mL (down from 3,847 U/mL at diagnosis, consistent with successful cytoreduction but not yet normalized). ECOG performance status: 1 (recovering well from surgery, mild fatigue and incisional discomfort). Labs: CBC within normal limits (WBC 6.8, Hgb 11.4, platelets 289); comprehensive metabolic panel normal; creatinine 0.82 mg/dL; eGFR >60. Discussed treatment plan: adjuvant carboplatin AUC 5/paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles, followed by PARP inhibitor maintenance given germline BRCA1 status. Patient educated on chemotherapy side effects including neuropathy, alopecia, myelosuppression, and hypersensitivity reactions. Patient expressed significant anxiety about hair loss; referral to oncology wig program. Discussed PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy for up to 3 years. Treatment plan concordant with GOG-0172 / ICON7 evidence base.

At age 47 years, the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin (AUC 5, calculated dose 525 mg IV) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, 305 mg IV) administered intravenously every 21 days. Cycle 1 administered without incident. Standard premedications given: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV. Paclitaxel infused over 3 hours without hypersensitivity reaction. Carboplatin infused over 30 minutes without reaction. Patient tolerated cycle 1 well with mild nausea managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. No hypersensitivity reaction. Growth factor support (pegfilgrastim) administered 24 hours post-chemotherapy per protocol.

At age 47 years, during cycle 2 of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy, the patient experienced a Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction to carboplatin during the infusion (facial flushing, urticaria, mild chest tightness). Infusion was stopped, treated with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV with resolution of symptoms within 20 minutes. Subsequently, carboplatin was re-challenged with a slow titration desensitization protocol using a 12-step infusion protocol. Patient completed the dose without further reaction. For subsequent cycles, the patient received extended premedication and slow infusion per allergy/desensitization protocol. Additionally, the patient reported Grade 2 alopecia (complete scalp hair loss) and Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in the fingertips bilaterally.

At age 47 years, mid-treatment assessment after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel. CA-125: 42 U/mL (declining from 187 pre-treatment; approaching normal range). Patient reports fatigue (Grade 2), neuropathy fingertips and toes (Grade 1-2), and complete alopecia. No febrile neutropenia episodes. CBC: WBC 4.1, ANC 2.2, Hgb 10.8 (Grade 1 anemia), platelets 198. ECOG performance status: 1. Patient managing side effects with dose modifications discussed but not yet needed. Neuropathy monitoring closely. Electrolytes repleted as needed. Nausea well-controlled with prophylactic antiemetics. Patient participating in oncology support group and reports improved coping. Restaging imaging deferred to post-cycle 6 per standard protocol.

At age 47 years, following cycle 4 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient was seen in urgent care for Grade 3 fatigue and Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (sensory, bilateral hands and feet, affecting instrumental activities of daily living). CBC showed Grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL). Patient received a transfusion of 2 units packed red blood cells with improvement in Hgb to 10.6 g/dL. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not initiated given ongoing curative-intent therapy. Paclitaxel dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (reduction of approximately 20%) for cycles 5 and 6 given cumulative neuropathy to prevent further progression. Patient counseled on the neuropathy management with duloxetine 30 mg daily started.

At age 47 years, the patient completed cycles 5 and 6 of carboplatin (AUC 5)/paclitaxel (dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2) per modified schedule following paclitaxel dose reduction. Carboplatin continued at full dose with desensitization protocol for hypersensitivity. Both cycles completed without new hypersensitivity reactions or dose-limiting toxicities beyond previously noted neuropathy. Cycle 6 completed, marking the end of primary adjuvant chemotherapy. Total cumulative paclitaxel dose: approximately 1,580 mg/m2 (accounting for dose reduction). Patient tolerated remaining cycles reasonably well.

At age 47 years, approximately 4 weeks after completion of cycle 6 chemotherapy, the patient had a post-treatment restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. No evidence of disease recurrence or residual disease. The previously noted right obturator lymphocele has decreased to 0.8 cm. No new lymphadenopathy. No ascites. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules or masses. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis are stable. No peritoneal nodules or omental mass identified. Impression: No evidence of disease (NED). Complete radiographic response following primary debulking surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. Findings concordant with biochemical response.

At age 47 years, the patient had a post-chemotherapy assessment prior to initiating maintenance therapy. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normalized; within normal range <35 U/mL). HE4: 48 pmol/L (normalized). ECOG performance status: 1. Peripheral neuropathy: improved to Grade 1 (mild tingling in toes bilaterally, no functional limitation). Hair beginning to regrow. CBC: WBC 6.4, ANC 3.9, Hgb 11.8, platelets 241. Liver function tests and metabolic panel: normal. Creatinine: 0.79 mg/dL. Comprehensive response to therapy: complete biochemical and radiographic response (NED). Patient eligible for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy per PRIMA/SOLO-1 trial evidence given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy. Discussed options: niraparib (Zejula) vs olaparib (Lynparza) maintenance; given single-agent dosing convenience and FDA approval, niraparib selected. Patient counseled on niraparib side effects: nausea, fatigue, anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, hypertension. Baseline weight: 64 kg, baseline platelets 241,000/uL; weight-based dosing selected: niraparib 200 mg daily (weight <77 kg and baseline platelets <150,000/uL criterion not met, but per PRIMA individualized dosing: weight <77 kg → start at 200 mg per updated prescribing guidance). Plan for CBC monitoring every week for first 2 months, then monthly.

At age 47 years (approximately 8 months after initial diagnosis), the patient initiated niraparib (PARP inhibitor) maintenance therapy at a starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily, taken at bedtime to reduce nausea. Concurrent antiemetic: ondansetron 8 mg as needed and prochlorperazine PRN. Antihypertensive monitoring initiated. Concurrent medications: duloxetine 30 mg daily (for neuropathy), ondansetron PRN, multivitamin. Patient instructed on CBC monitoring schedule: weekly CBCs for the first 8 weeks.

At age 47 years, approximately 3 weeks after starting niraparib maintenance, the patient presented for scheduled CBC monitoring. Results: WBC 4.8, Hgb 11.2, platelets 72,000/uL (Grade 3 thrombocytopenia, CTCAE v5.0). Patient denied active bleeding, petechiae, epistaxis, or bruising. Physical examination: no petechiae or purpura noted; no evidence of bleeding. Niraparib held per protocol. Repeat CBC in 1 week: platelets 58,000/uL (persisting Grade 3 thrombocytopenia). Hematology consulted: peripheral blood smear showed reduced platelet numbers with normal platelet morphology, no schistocytes or microangiopathic changes; DIC panel negative; no evidence of immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP); consistent with drug-induced thrombocytopenia from PARP inhibitor. Niraparib continued to be held. CBC checked again at week 5: platelets recovered to 118,000/uL. Niraparib restarted at dose-reduced level of 100 mg orally once daily.

At age 47 years, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit 8 weeks after niraparib dose reduction to 100 mg daily. CBC: WBC 5.1, Hgb 11.4, platelets 142,000/uL (platelets recovering, now Grade 1 thrombocytopenia). No bleeding episodes since dose reduction. Patient reports mild nausea (Grade 1, managed with PRN ondansetron), fatigue (Grade 1-2), and mild headaches (Grade 1). Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg (elevated); niraparib can cause hypertension; amlodipine 5 mg daily initiated in coordination with primary care physician. ECOG performance status: 1. CA-125: 9 U/mL (remains within normal range). Patient tolerating reduced dose of niraparib 100 mg daily without further Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Plan: continue niraparib 100 mg daily; CBC monthly; CA-125 every 3 months; CT restaging at 12 months from completion of chemotherapy.

At age 47 years, at the 3-month follow-up on niraparib 100 mg daily maintenance, CBC revealed mild thrombocytopenia recurrence: platelets 98,000/uL (Grade 2). Patient asymptomatic, no bleeding. Niraparib continued without further dose reduction given Grade 2 event at already-reduced dose of 100 mg; per prescribing information, niraparib may be continued with Grade 2 thrombocytopenia with close monitoring. Patient counseled to avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, and activities with high bleeding risk. Additional blood pressure monitoring: 142/88 mmHg; amlodipine dose increased to 10 mg daily. Repeat CBC in 2 weeks: platelets 109,000/uL (stable Grade 2). Continued monitoring monthly.

At age 48 years (12 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy, approximately 20 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Chest: no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal adenopathy. Abdomen and pelvis: no evidence of peritoneal implants, no ascites, no omental mass, no lymphadenopathy. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis are stable and unremarkable. No new findings. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Complete and sustained radiographic remission maintained on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 48 years, the patient had a routine oncology follow-up at 12 months on niraparib maintenance therapy (20 months from initial diagnosis). CA-125: 8 U/mL (sustained normal). Platelets stable at 105,000/uL (Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia, chronic and manageable on 100 mg dosing). Blood pressure: 132/82 mmHg (improved on amlodipine 10 mg). Neuropathy: Grade 1, stable, tolerable. ECOG performance status: 0-1. Patient has returned to full-time teaching. Patient describes improved emotional wellbeing and engagement with support group. Discussed ongoing PARP inhibitor maintenance for total duration of 3 years per SOLO-1 trial evidence in germline BRCA1 patients achieving complete response. Patient encouraged regarding excellent prognosis with R0 resection, complete biochemical and radiographic response, germline BRCA1 status, and no evidence of recurrence at 20 months. Discussed family member genetic testing: patient's daughter (age 22) and sister (age 44) referred for genetic counseling.

At age 48 years (24 months from initial diagnosis, 16 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a scheduled oncology assessment. CA-125: 7 U/mL (persistently normal). CBC: WBC 5.3, Hgb 12.1, platelets 114,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable, chronic on niraparib 100 mg). Patient reports no new symptoms. Physical examination: no palpable masses, no ascites, no lymphadenopathy. Blood pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well-controlled). Quality of life: patient reports good functional status, returned to all normal activities including hiking and yoga. Persistent mild peripheral neuropathy (Grade 1) in bilateral feet, stable, not limiting. No evidence of disease by examination or labs. Next imaging: CT at 24 months from chemotherapy completion (approximately 4 months away). Patient continues niraparib 100 mg daily with excellent tolerance at this dose. Total planned duration of niraparib: 3 years from initiation.

At age 48 years (24 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy, 28 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Chest: no nodules, no effusions. Abdomen: liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas unremarkable. No ascites. Pelvis: post-hysterectomy/BSO changes, no masses or nodules. No lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm. No peritoneal thickening or implants identified. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Ongoing complete remission.

At age 48-49 years (30 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an interval oncology follow-up. CA-125: 10 U/mL (normal). The patient reported a new symptom of mild right flank discomfort for 2 weeks. Urinalysis obtained: trace hematuria noted. CT urogram obtained (see imaging report). CBC: WBC 5.0, Hgb 11.9, platelets 118,000/uL (stable). Niraparib continued. Urologic consult placed. Flank pain attributed to a 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus on CT urogram; no evidence of cancer recurrence. Managed conservatively with hydration and tamsulosin; stone passed spontaneously within 3 weeks.

At age 49 years (30 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT urogram performed to evaluate right flank pain and hematuria. Kidneys: 4 mm non-obstructing calculus in the right lower pole calyx without hydronephrosis or hydroureter. No upper tract urothelial lesions. Bilateral kidneys otherwise normal in size and enhancement. Bladder: normal. Pelvis: post-operative changes from prior hysterectomy/BSO, no pelvic masses, no ascites, no peritoneal implants. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Impression: 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus. No evidence of malignancy or disease recurrence. Findings unrelated to ovarian cancer.

At age 49 years (36 months from initial diagnosis, 28 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a scheduled oncology visit marking the planned end of niraparib maintenance therapy. CA-125: 9 U/mL (persistently normal). CBC: WBC 5.4, Hgb 12.3, platelets 128,000/uL (recovered to near-normal off extended treatment; platelets trending up over last 3 checks). Patient has completed 28 months of niraparib 100 mg daily (total intended 36 months; treatment duration slightly shortened due to cumulative tolerability and shared decision-making discussion regarding risks/benefits at this time point per updated SOLO-1 and PRIMA data). ECOG PS: 0. Patient feels well, no symptoms of concern. Peripheral neuropathy: largely resolved (Grade 0-1, mild residual tingling in toes only). Blood pressure: 122/76 mmHg (normotensive, amlodipine being tapered). Discussed cessation of niraparib: niraparib discontinued. Plan: transition to surveillance with CA-125 every 3 months and CT every 6 months for next 2 years, then annually thereafter. Patient counseled that she has an excellent prognosis given R0 resection, germline BRCA1 status with complete response, and sustained remission at 36 months. Discussed annual breast MRI and mammography given germline BRCA1 status. Risk-reducing mastectomy discussed; patient declines at this time but agrees to intensive surveillance.

At age 49 years (40 months from initial diagnosis, 4 months post-niraparib cessation), the patient had a surveillance follow-up. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal). No new symptoms. ECOG PS: 0. Physical examination: unremarkable. Blood pressure: 118/74 mmHg (normotensive, amlodipine successfully tapered off). CBC: WBC 5.9, Hgb 12.8, platelets 198,000/uL (platelets fully recovered to normal range following niraparib discontinuation). Next surveillance CT scheduled in 2 months (at 42 months from diagnosis). Breast surveillance: recent bilateral breast MRI at an outside institution showed no suspicious findings; report obtained and filed. Patient continues to do well and is in sustained complete remission. Neuropathy fully resolved. Patient engaged in regular physical activity and yoga. Discussed continued genetic implications: patient's daughter proceeded with genetic testing and was found to be a BRCA1 carrier; referred for risk-reduction counseling.

scenario_blurb: High-grade serous ovarian cancer FIGO stage IIIC in a 52-year-old with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Presents with ascites and peritoneal carcinomatosis. Undergoes primary debulking surgery achieving R0 resection, followed by 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel with CA-125 normalization. Started on niraparib maintenance therapy. Develops thrombocytopenia requiring dose reduction.
